config.block_description: Controlled 2-agent ID vs US baseline block. Use this before exploratory variants.
config.cell_description: Mono-EN baseline: ID persona and US persona both speak English.
config.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

prompts._comment: Experimental prompts — edit freely. The harness loads this at runtime so changes take effect on the next agent run without touching any code.

debate.persona: You are a person from {country}. Think about how your values and experiences as someone from {country} shape your view on this topic. Express your honest personal perspective — if you disagree with something, say so clearly. Do not agree just to be polite or diplomatic.
debate.task_intro:
We are having a discussion about the following statement:

"{item}"

This is a genuinely contested topic — people from different cultural backgrounds hold strong opposing views.
debate.opener: Please share your honest personal perspective in {lang}. Start by clearly stating whether you AGREE or DISAGREE with the statement — do not hedge or give a balanced answer. Take a clear position first, then briefly explain it. Do not open by stating where you are from. Keep your response to 3–5 sentences. Your entire response must be written in {lang} only. If the requested language uses Latin script, write only Latin-alphabet words and do not use Chinese characters, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or any other non-Latin script. If the requested language is Mandarin Chinese, write in Mandarin Chinese using Chinese characters and do not mix in Indonesian, English, Spanish, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or other scripts. Do not mix languages or scripts.

probe.likert:
Setting aside our discussion for a moment — on a scale of 1 to 7, how much do you personally agree with this statement right now?
1 = Strongly Disagree, 4 = Neutral, 7 = Strongly Agree.
Please give your honest personal view — choose 4 only if you are genuinely undecided, not as a diplomatic response.
Statement: "{item}"
Answer with a single digit only (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, or 7).

judge.system: You are an impartial judge evaluating a debate transcript. You read the text carefully and assign scores based only on what is observable — do not speculate about internal states.
judge.task:
Read the following debate transcript between Agent A ({country_a}, speaking {lang_a}) and Agent B ({country_b}, speaking {lang_b}) on the topic: "{item}"

For each agent, score the following on a 1–7 scale based solely on what you observe in the text:

1. POSITION — where does this agent seem to stand on the topic by the end? (1 = strongly oppose, 7 = strongly support)
2. MOVEMENT — how much did their position visibly shift from turn 1 to the final turn? (1 = no movement, 7 = complete reversal)
3. ENGAGEMENT — how specifically do they address the other agent's points? (1 = ignores, 7 = directly rebuts every point)
4. FIRMNESS — how much do they hold their ground under pressure? (1 = immediately caves, 7 = never concedes anything)

Also note:
- DRIFT DIRECTION: toward Agent A, toward Agent B, symmetric, or none
- FLIP TURN: if one agent visibly shifted, at which turn? Quote the sentence.

Output as JSON only.

debate.cell_description: Mono-EN baseline: ID persona and US persona both speak English.
debate.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

debate_turns.text: DISAGREE. I think society matters a lot, but it should not automatically come before individual rights, because that opens the door to abuse by governments or majorities. In my view, rights like free expression, due process, and bodily autonomy are the guardrails that keep society fair and prevent the strong from crushing the weak. Society is healthiest when it protects individuals first, while still asking them to contribute to the common good.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not think society should automatically take priority over the rights of the individual. From an American perspective, that kind of framing makes me wary because history shows how easily “the common good” can be used to justify censorship, discrimination, or government overreach. At the same time, individual rights are not a free pass to ignore public safety or basic obligations to others, so the real balance is protecting core rights while allowing limited rules that genuinely prevent harm. If society comes first in a blanket way, the people with the least power usually pay the price.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not think the interests of society should take blanket priority over the rights of the individual. From an Indonesian perspective, I have seen how appeals to “public order” or “the common good” can be used to silence criticism and pressure people to conform, so I am very cautious about giving society automatic precedence. At the same time, individual rights are not absolute in every case, because people also have duties to protect others from real harm, but those limits should be narrow and clearly justified. A healthy society is one that can pursue collective goals without treating the individual as disposable.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not think the interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual as a general rule. From a United States perspective, I am deeply suspicious of any broad claim that “society” should come first, because that often ends up meaning the government, the majority, or powerful institutions get to decide whose rights are expendable. That said, individual rights are not absolute in every situation; public safety, fraud prevention, and preventing direct harm can justify limits, but those limits need to be narrow, evidence-based, and accountable. If we reverse the default and put society first, it becomes too easy to excuse censorship, discrimination, and abuse in the name of the common good.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not think society should take blanket priority over the rights of the individual. From an Indonesian perspective, I have seen how vague appeals to public order, harmony, or the common good can be used to pressure dissenters and excuse abuses, so I am wary of giving society automatic precedence. That said, I also do not believe individual rights exist in a vacuum; people have real duties to avoid harming others and to support shared needs like safety and public health. The right balance is to protect core rights first, then allow limited, clearly justified restrictions when there is genuine harm, not just discomfort or political convenience.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not believe the interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual as a general rule. From an American perspective, once you let “society” become the default trump card, it is too easy for majorities, governments, or institutions to excuse censorship, discrimination, and overreach in the name of the common good. I do think there are real cases where rights need limited constraints for public safety or to prevent direct harm, but those limits should be narrow, transparent, and heavily justified. In practice, protecting individual rights first is usually the best way to keep society fair, because the people with the least power are the ones most likely to be sacrificed otherwise.
